source.sourcePolicy: Rules and flow are compiled only from the jurisdiction reference/source documents supplied in the Nationwide archive, plus the user-supplied Nebraska reference.

Following decriminalization, Oregon greatly expanded set-asides for marijuana offenses. For set-aside

questions.prompt: Are you asking about your own record?
options: I am helping someone complete their own check

questions.prompt: Is this a state or local matter from Oregon, rather than a federal matter?
How did the Oregon case or record end?
options: Arrest or citation with no charge filed
Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted

questions.prompt: What level or type was the charge?
Do any of these Oregon descriptions sound close to your situation?
options: Set-aside of arrests or charges without conviction under ORS 137.225(1)(c)
Set-aside of eligible convictions under ORS 137.225(1)(a)
None of these / I am not sure
questions.helper: This answer is optional and does not select your legal pathway. The backend uses the factual answers and state rules.

questions.prompt: Have you completed every part of the sentence, supervision, probation, parole, classes, or community service?
Are all fines, restitution, court costs, and other required financial obligations resolved?
Do you currently have any pending criminal charge, open case, probation, parole, or other supervision?
When was the case disposed of, dismissed, completed, or discharged?
What Oregon county, parish, borough, district, or local filing area handled the case?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What charge, statute, or record description appears on the record?
Does the record involve any Oregon category that the source identifies as excluded or review-required?
Do you have court paperwork or a criminal-history record showing the exact charge and outcome?
Where in Oregon was the case handled?
What case, docket, cause, or arrest number appears on the record?

caseOutcomeOptions.label: Arrest or citation with no charge filed
Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted

pathways.label: Set-aside of arrests or charges without conviction under ORS 137.225(1)(c)
pathways.summary: The most accessible relief, with short or no waiting periods: Dismissed charge — may be set aside, generally with little or no waiting period (but see the drafting-quirk caution: some dismissed serious charges have at times been forced to wait the same period as a conviction). Acquittal (found not guilty) — may be set aside at any time. Arrest with no charges filed — may be set aside 60 days after the prosecutor notifies the court that no charge will be pursued (SB 397 cut this from one year). No requirement (post-SB 397) of being arrest-free for three years or conviction-free for ten before applying to set aside an uncharged arrest.
ruleClauses: The most accessible relief, with short or no waiting periods:

Dismissed charge — may be set aside, generally with little or no waiting period (but see the drafting-quirk caution: some dismissed serious charges have at times been forced to wait the same period as a conviction).
Acquittal (found not guilty) — may be set aside at any time.
Arrest with no charges filed — may be set aside 60 days after the prosecutor notifies the court that no charge will be pursued (SB 397 cut this from one year).
No requirement (post-SB 397) of being arrest-free for three years or conviction-free for ten before applying to set aside an uncharged arrest.
One statute, two broad situations, routed by disposition.
Arrests and charges without conviction (137.225(1)(c))
Convictions (137.225(1)(a))
A person convicted of an eligible offense who has FULLY completed the sentence (all incarceration, probation,
post-prison supervision, and restitution) may move to set aside the conviction after the applicable waiting
period (Section 2), provided they have no new conviction during that period. Eligible offenses include most
misdemeanors, Class C felonies, many Class B felonies, Class A marijuana/racketeering in some cases,
Schedule I drug-possession offenses, and violations. A person still under supervision has not completed the
sentence and is not yet eligible.
Following decriminalization, Oregon greatly expanded set-asides for marijuana offenses. For set-aside
purposes, marijuana offenses committed before July 1, 2015 are reclassified to the level they would carry today,
which makes many of them eligible far sooner (or immediately), and certain old municipal/justice-court
marijuana-possession judgments have been given expiration relief by statute.
Route by disposition. Dismissal → set aside with little/no wait; acquittal → any time; uncharged arrest →
60 days after the DA declines; conviction → complete the sentence, then the class-based wait.
For marijuana, reclassify pre-July-2015 offenses to today's level before assessing eligibility — it often
shortens or eliminates the wait.
Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase
Arrests and charges without conviction (137.225(1)(c)) The most accessible relief, with short or no waiting periods:
waitingRules: The most accessible relief, with short or no waiting periods:

Dismissed charge — may be set aside, generally with little or no waiting period (but see the drafting-quirk caution: some dismissed serious charges have at times been forced to wait the same period as a conviction).
Arrest with no charges filed — may be set aside 60 days after the prosecutor notifies the court that no charge will be pursued (SB 397 cut this from one year).
No requirement (post-SB 397) of being arrest-free for three years or conviction-free for ten before applying to set aside an uncharged arrest.

Class B or C misdemeanor, 1 year Conviction/finding or release, later

exclusionRules: Oregon excludes whole categories regardless of time elapsed or sentence completion (ORS 137.225(5), (6),
Traffic offenses, including standard DUII convictions — categorically ineligible (a DUII diversion that is dismissed is treated differently, but a DUII conviction cannot be set aside).
Sex offenses requiring registration under ORS 163A.010 and most other sex crimes.
Class A felonies (with limited marijuana-related exceptions).
Serious person crimes / violent offenses — murder, manslaughter, criminally negligent homicide, assault causing serious physical injury, and similar.
New conviction during the waiting period (other than a same-episode conviction) resets eligibility.
Pending charges. A person under charge for any crime when the motion is pending is not eligible.
Sentence not complete. Still being on probation or post-prison supervision (or owing restitution) means the sentence is not complete and the clock has not started.
Federal or out-of-state convictions are not reachable by ORS 137.225.
Firearms. As of 2024 Oregon State Police policy, a set-aside does NOT restore firearm rights; a separate restoration process is required.
The eligible/ineligible line in ORS 137.225(5)–(6) is defined by offense and class and has been
amended repeatedly (2021 SB 397, plus 2024–2025 changes), and a known drafting quirk in
subsection (9) has at times forced some DISMISSED serious charges to wait the full conviction
The agent must map the offense to its ORS class, check the current eligibility lists, and verify the dismissed-charge timing before declaring eligibility.
Traffic/DUII, registrable sex offenses, Class A felonies, and serious person crimes are the hard
Set expectations on firearms: a set-aside seals the record but, under current OSP policy, does not
Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

Oregon excludes whole categories regardless of time elapsed or sentence completion (ORS 137.225(5), (6), (10)). • Traffic offenses, including standard DUII convictions — categorically ineligible (a DUII diversion that is dismissed is treated differently, but a DUII conviction cannot be set aside). • Sex offenses requiring registration under ORS 163A.010 and most other sex crimes. • Class A felonies (with limited marijuana-related exceptions). • Serious person crimes / violent offenses — murder, manslaughter, criminally negligent homicide, assault causing serious physical injury, and similar. • Aggravated vehicular homicide and other enumerated serious offenses. • Crimes against vulnerable victims (certain offenses against children or dependent persons). • Criminal mistreatment and certain offenses against family/household members, as enumerated.

pathways.summary: purposes, marijuana offenses committed before July 1, 2015 are reclassified to the level they would carry today, which makes many of them eligible far sooner (or immediately), and certain old municipal/justice-court marijuana-possession judgments have been given expiration relief by statute. Route by disposition. Dismissal → set aside with little/no wait; acquittal → any time; uncharged arrest → 60 days after the DA declines; conviction → complete the sentence, then the class-based wait.

A pre-7/1/2015 marijuana offense is reclassified to the level
it would carry today for set-aside purposes, which often
makes it eligible immediately or after a much shorter wait.
Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase
The motion is accompanied by a proposed Order to Set Aside. On entry, the order directs that the record of the
arrest, citation, charge, or conviction be set aside and sealed, directs the Oregon State Police to seal its records,
and provides that under ORS 137.225(8)(b) the offense is deemed not to have occurred.
This reference is an internal training and retrieval aid for the Wilma assistant. It summarizes Oregon's set-aside statute and
in 2024–2025; a drafting quirk has affected some dismissed serious charges, and a set-aside does not restore firearm rights under
current OSP policy. The agent must verify the current statutory text, the waiting-period tiers, the eligible/excluded offense lists,
OSP request processes, and county procedures before relying on any rule here.
One statute, one remedy. Oregon has no separate sealing vs. expungement tracks — ORS 137.225 sets aside AND seals, and the offense is then deemed never to have occurred.

SB 397 reset the timelines. Effective 1/1/2022 it cut most waits (e.g., Class B felony to 7 years), removed most filing fees, and ended the old flat 10-year wait for anyone with multiple convictions — older guides are unreliable.
Same-episode convictions don't disqualify. A second conviction from the same criminal episode is not a disqualifying “other conviction” during the waiting period.

The dismissed-serious-charge quirk. A drafting error in subsection (9) has at times forced some dismissed serious charges to wait the full conviction period rather than being set aside promptly — verify current treatment.
Firearms not restored. Per 2024 OSP policy, a set-aside does not restore firearm rights; a separate restoration is required.
Fingerprint + OSP step is mandatory for convictions and is the main source of delay.
Processing times vary widely by county — some have run close to two years.
Marijuana reclassification. Pre-July-2015 marijuana offenses are reclassified to today's level for set-aside purposes, often making them eligible immediately.
Rely on the current statute, not older summaries — SB 397 and the 2024–2025 amendments changed
the waits and eligibility substantially.

Effect of relief: when the court grants the motion, it enters an order setting aside and sealing the record, and
under ORS 137.225(8)(b) the arrest, citation, charge, or conviction is “deemed not to have occurred.” The
person may then lawfully answer that they were never arrested, cited, or convicted of that offense on most

conviction (same-episode convictions excepted).

person must have no other conviction during the period. SB 397 cut these substantially.

exclusionRules.ruleText: Oregon excludes whole categories regardless of time elapsed or sentence completion (ORS 137.225(5), (6),
Traffic offenses, including standard DUII convictions — categorically ineligible (a DUII diversion that is dismissed is treated differently, but a DUII conviction cannot be set aside).
Sex offenses requiring registration under ORS 163A.010 and most other sex crimes.
Class A felonies (with limited marijuana-related exceptions).
Serious person crimes / violent offenses — murder, manslaughter, criminally negligent homicide, assault causing serious physical injury, and similar.
Aggravated vehicular homicide and other enumerated serious offenses.
Crimes against vulnerable victims (certain offenses against children or dependent persons).
Criminal mistreatment and certain offenses against family/household members, as enumerated.
New conviction during the waiting period (other than a same-episode conviction) resets eligibility.
Pending charges. A person under charge for any crime when the motion is pending is not eligible.
Sentence not complete. Still being on probation or post-prison supervision (or owing restitution) means the sentence is not complete and the clock has not started.
Federal or out-of-state convictions are not reachable by ORS 137.225.
Firearms. As of 2024 Oregon State Police policy, a set-aside does NOT restore firearm rights; a separate restoration process is required.

subsection (9) has at times forced some DISMISSED serious charges to wait the full conviction
The agent must map the offense to its ORS class, check the current eligibility lists, and verify the dismissed-charge timing before declaring eligibility.
Traffic/DUII, registrable sex offenses, Class A felonies, and serious person crimes are the hard
categorical bars — screen for these first; they don't clear with time.
Set expectations on firearms: a set-aside seals the record but, under current OSP policy, does not
restore gun rights — that needs a separate process.
Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

Oregon excludes whole categories regardless of time elapsed or sentence completion (ORS 137.225(5), (6), (10)). • Traffic offenses, including standard DUII convictions — categorically ineligible (a DUII diversion that is dismissed is treated differently, but a DUII conviction cannot be set aside). • Sex offenses requiring registration under ORS 163A.010 and most other sex crimes. • Class A felonies (with limited marijuana-related exceptions). • Serious person crimes / violent offenses — murder, manslaughter, criminally negligent homicide, assault causing serious physical injury, and similar. • Aggravated vehicular homicide and other enumerated serious offenses. • Crimes against vulnerable victims (certain offenses against children or dependent persons). • Criminal mistreatment and certain offenses against family/household members, as enumerated.
A person under charge for any crime when the motion is pending is not eligible. • Sentence not complete.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING The eligible/ineligible line in ORS 137.225(5)–(6) is defined by offense and class and has been amended repeatedly (2021 SB 397, plus 2024–2025 changes), and a known drafting quirk in subsection (9) has at times forced some DISMISSED serious charges to wait the full conviction period.

copyGuardrails: Use Oregon-specific remedy terminology returned by the profile; do not relabel every remedy as expungement.
Use “may qualify,” “possible path,” and “the court or agency makes the final decision.”
Do not promise erasure, sealing, eligibility, timing, filing acceptance, or a court outcome.
Do not show checkout unless the backend returns a packet-ready result and paymentAllowed=true.

sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules
sourceSections.text:
One statute, two broad situations, routed by disposition.

Situation A — Arrests and charges without conviction (137.225(1)(c))
The most accessible relief, with short or no waiting periods:

• Dismissed charge — may be set aside, generally with little or no waiting period (but see the drafting-quirk
caution: some dismissed serious charges have at times been forced to wait the same period as a conviction).
• Acquittal (found not guilty) — may be set aside at any time.
• Arrest with no charges filed — may be set aside 60 days after the prosecutor notifies the court that no
charge will be pursued (SB 397 cut this from one year).
• No requirement (post-SB 397) of being arrest-free for three years or conviction-free for ten before applying to
set aside an uncharged arrest.

Situation B — Convictions (137.225(1)(a))
A person convicted of an eligible offense who has FULLY completed the sentence (all incarceration, probation,
post-prison supervision, and restitution) may move to set aside the conviction after the applicable waiting
period (Section 2), provided they have no new conviction during that period. Eligible offenses include most
misdemeanors, Class C felonies, many Class B felonies, Class A marijuana/racketeering in some cases,
Schedule I drug-possession offenses, and violations. A person still under supervision has not completed the
sentence and is not yet eligible.

Marijuana relief
Following decriminalization, Oregon greatly expanded set-asides for marijuana offenses. For set-aside
purposes, marijuana offenses committed before July 1, 2015 are reclassified to the level they would carry today,
which makes many of them eligible far sooner (or immediately), and certain old municipal/justice-court
marijuana-possession judgments have been given expiration relief by statute.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Route by disposition. Dismissal → set aside with little/no wait; acquittal → any time; uncharged arrest →
60 days after the DA declines; conviction → complete the sentence, then the class-based wait.
For marijuana, reclassify pre-July-2015 offenses to today's level before assessing eligibility — it often
shortens or eliminates the wait.

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
Conviction waits run from the date of conviction or release from imprisonment, whichever is later, and the
person must have no other conviction during the period. SB 397 cut these substantially.

Disposition / offense class Waiting period Measured from

Dismissed charge Little / none Dismissal (verify the serious-charge quirk)

Acquittal None (any time) —

Arrest, no charges filed 60 days DA's notice declining to prosecute

Class B or C misdemeanor, 1 year Conviction/finding or release, later
violation, contempt finding

Class A misdemeanor 3 years Conviction or release, later

Class C felony 5 years Conviction or release, later

Class B felony 7 years Conviction or release, later

A central SB 397 improvement is how MULTIPLE convictions are treated. The single waiting period is
measured from the most recent eligible conviction, and — importantly — a conviction arising from the SAME
criminal episode (or same course of conduct) as the offense being set aside does NOT count as a disqualifying
“other conviction” during the waiting period. The old regime forced a flat 10-year wait whenever a person had
more than one conviction, even from one case; that is gone. The waiting period is keyed to the highest
applicable class among the convictions a person seeks to set aside.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Use the class tiers: 1 yr (Class B/C misdemeanor, violation, contempt), 3 yrs (Class A misdemeanor), 5
yrs (Class C felony), 7 yrs (Class B felony), measured from conviction or release, whichever is later.
Don't let a same-episode second conviction scare a user off — SB 397 says it doesn't count as a
disqualifying other conviction. Confirm sentence completion (including restitution) before the clock starts.

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying / ineligible offenses
sourceSections.text:
Oregon excludes whole categories regardless of time elapsed or sentence completion (ORS 137.225(5), (6),
(10)).

• Traffic offenses, including standard DUII convictions — categorically ineligible (a DUII diversion that is
dismissed is treated differently, but a DUII conviction cannot be set aside).
• Sex offenses requiring registration under ORS 163A.010 and most other sex crimes.
• Class A felonies (with limited marijuana-related exceptions).
• Serious person crimes / violent offenses — murder, manslaughter, criminally negligent homicide, assault
causing serious physical injury, and similar.
• Aggravated vehicular homicide and other enumerated serious offenses.
• Crimes against vulnerable victims (certain offenses against children or dependent persons).
• Criminal mistreatment and certain offenses against family/household members, as enumerated.

Record / timing disqualifiers
• New conviction during the waiting period (other than a same-episode conviction) resets eligibility.
• Pending charges. A person under charge for any crime when the motion is pending is not eligible.
• Sentence not complete. Still being on probation or post-prison supervision (or owing restitution) means the
sentence is not complete and the clock has not started.
• Federal or out-of-state convictions are not reachable by ORS 137.225.

Important effect limit
• Firearms. As of 2024 Oregon State Police policy, a set-aside does NOT restore firearm rights; a separate
restoration process is required.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
The eligible/ineligible line in ORS 137.225(5)–(6) is defined by offense and class and has been
amended repeatedly (2021 SB 397, plus 2024–2025 changes), and a known drafting quirk in
subsection (9) has at times forced some DISMISSED serious charges to wait the full conviction
period. The agent must map the offense to its ORS class, check the current eligibility lists, and
verify the dismissed-charge timing before declaring eligibility.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Traffic/DUII, registrable sex offenses, Class A felonies, and serious person crimes are the hard
categorical bars — screen for these first; they don't clear with time.
Set expectations on firearms: a set-aside seals the record but, under current OSP policy, does not
restore gun rights — that needs a separate process.

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Case outcome rules
sourceSections.text:
Disposition and offense class drive the timing and eligibility. This table is the agent's primary routing logic for
Oregon.

Case outcome Route Waiting period Note

Charge dismissed Set aside Little / none Verify serious-charge quirk

Acquittal (not guilty) Set aside Any time —

Arrest, no charges filed Set aside 60 days After DA declines

Class B/C misdemeanor, Set aside 1 year Eligible offense
violation, contempt

Class A misdemeanor Set aside 3 years Eligible offense

Class C felony Set aside 5 years Eligible offense

Class B felony Set aside 7 years Eligible (e.g., many drug felonies)

Marijuana (pre-7/1/2015) Set aside Reclassified Often immediate / shorter

Traffic / DUII / sex-reg. / — Ineligible Categorical bar
Class A felony / person crime

Effect of relief: when the court grants the motion, it enters an order setting aside and sealing the record, and
under ORS 137.225(8)(b) the arrest, citation, charge, or conviction is “deemed not to have occurred.” The
person may then lawfully answer that they were never arrested, cited, or convicted of that offense on most
applications. The order reaches Oregon court and law-enforcement records (OSP/LEDS); it does not reach
federal or other states' records, and — per current OSP policy — it does not restore firearm rights.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Tell users the set-aside is strong: the event is legally deemed never to have occurred and they may deny
it on most applications. But it reaches only Oregon records and doesn't restore firearm rights.
Match the waiting tier to the highest offense class in the motion, and confirm no disqualifying new
conviction (same-episode convictions excepted).

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
Oregon relief is by a motion to set aside filed in the circuit court where the conviction was entered or the
citation issued. The Oregon Judicial Department and many county courts publish set-aside packets; a State
Police fingerprint/record step is required for convictions.

Core components of a set-aside filing
1 Motion and Affidavit to Set Aside (ORS 137.225) — identifies the case, the offense, the disposition, the
statutory basis, and recites the eligibility facts (sentence completed, waiting period elapsed, no new
conviction, no pending charges).

2 Proposed Order to Set Aside — the order the judge signs; on entry the record is set aside and sealed and the
offense is deemed not to have occurred.
3 Fingerprint card + Oregon State Police record check — for convictions, a set of fingerprints is submitted
to the OSP with the statutory fee so the State Police can confirm the criminal history; the OSP and DA
receive copies of the motion.

4 Certificate of completion / proof of sentence completion where needed (e.g., probation discharge), and
proof of service on the prosecuting attorney.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Use the county's set-aside packet where available; pair the Motion/Affidavit with the proposed Order, and
for convictions include the OSP fingerprint card and fee so the State Police can run the record check.
Serve the prosecuting attorney and keep proof; the DA has a window to object and may request a
hearing.

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
1 Get the record. Obtain your Oregon State Police (LEDS) criminal history and the court disposition to
confirm the offense class, the disposition, sentence-completion dates, and any other convictions.

2 Confirm eligibility. Arrest/charge vs. conviction; the class-based waiting period; sentence completion
(including restitution and supervision); no disqualifying new conviction; and that the offense is not
categorically excluded.

3 Prepare the Motion, Affidavit, and proposed Order citing ORS 137.225, with the eligibility recitals; for
convictions, complete the OSP fingerprint card.

4 File in the circuit court where the conviction was entered or the citation issued, and submit the fingerprints
+ fee to the Oregon State Police (SB 397 eliminated the court filing fee for many set-asides; a residual
OSP/processing fee around $33 may apply per conviction).
5 Serve the prosecuting attorney (district attorney or city attorney) with a copy of the motion.
6 DA review / hearing. The prosecutor has a statutory period to object; if there is no objection many courts
grant on the papers, and if the DA objects the court holds a hearing.

7 Order and sealing. If granted, the court enters the Order to Set Aside; the clerk and the Oregon State Police
seal the record and the offense is deemed not to have occurred. Keep a certified copy of the order.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Venue is the circuit court of the county where the conviction was entered or the citation issued — a
person with cases in several counties files separately in each.
The OSP fingerprint/record step is the usual bottleneck and processing can take many months in some
counties; start the LEDS request and fingerprinting early.

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Court-specific quirks
sourceSections.text:
• One statute, one remedy. Oregon has no separate sealing vs. expungement tracks — ORS 137.225 sets aside
AND seals, and the offense is then deemed never to have occurred.
• SB 397 reset the timelines. Effective 1/1/2022 it cut most waits (e.g., Class B felony to 7 years), removed
most filing fees, and ended the old flat 10-year wait for anyone with multiple convictions — older guides are
unreliable.
• Same-episode convictions don't disqualify. A second conviction from the same criminal episode is not a
disqualifying “other conviction” during the waiting period.
• The dismissed-serious-charge quirk. A drafting error in subsection (9) has at times forced some dismissed
serious charges to wait the full conviction period rather than being set aside promptly — verify current
treatment.
• Firearms not restored. Per 2024 OSP policy, a set-aside does not restore firearm rights; a separate
restoration is required.
• Fingerprint + OSP step is mandatory for convictions and is the main source of delay.
• Processing times vary widely by county — some have run close to two years.
• Marijuana reclassification. Pre-July-2015 marijuana offenses are reclassified to today's level for set-aside
purposes, often making them eligible immediately.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Rely on the current statute, not older summaries — SB 397 and the 2024–2025 amendments changed
the waits and eligibility substantially.
Flag two expectations up front: long county processing times, and that a set-aside won't restore gun
rights.

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Fee information
sourceSections.text:
Item Amount Note

Court filing fee (many set-asides) $0 SB 397 eliminated most court filing fees

OSP fingerprint / processing fee ~$33 per conviction Residual fee for the State Police record check

Arrest / dismissed-charge set-aside Often $0 No court fee

Oregon State Police (LEDS) record OSP fee To confirm record before filing

Attorney fees (if represented) Varies Not required; legal-aid and set-aside clinics assist

SB 397 made Oregon set-asides far cheaper by eliminating most court filing fees (a reported saving of roughly
$281 per case). What typically remains is the Oregon State Police fingerprint/processing fee (around $33 per
conviction) for the record check, plus the cost of obtaining the LEDS criminal history to confirm eligibility.
Set-asides of arrests and dismissed charges are often free. Legal-aid organizations and periodic set-aside clinics
assist people who cannot afford a lawyer.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Lead with the good news: most court filing fees were eliminated by SB 397, so the main cost is the ~$33
OSP processing fee per conviction plus the record-check fee.
Arrest and dismissed-charge set-asides are usually free — cost should rarely deter a non-conviction
filing.

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Criminal history (State Police) request process
sourceSections.text:
Confirming the record first establishes the offense class (which sets the waiting tier), the disposition, and the
sentence-completion dates that start the clock.

sourceSections.title: More than five (5) years have elapsed since conviction/
sourceSections.text:
release (the Class C felony waiting period).
5. Defendant has not been convicted of any other offense during
the waiting period (any same-episode conviction does not
count), and has no criminal charge pending.

sourceSections.title: MARIJUANA (pre-July 1, 2015):
sourceSections.text:
A pre-7/1/2015 marijuana offense is reclassified to the level
it would carry today for set-aside purposes, which often
makes it eligible immediately or after a much shorter wait.

Order to Set Aside

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

The motion is accompanied by a proposed Order to Set Aside. On entry, the order directs that the record of the
arrest, citation, charge, or conviction be set aside and sealed, directs the Oregon State Police to seal its records,
and provides that under ORS 137.225(8)(b) the offense is deemed not to have occurred.

This reference is an internal training and retrieval aid for the Wilma assistant. It summarizes Oregon's set-aside statute and
in 2024–2025; a drafting quirk has affected some dismissed serious charges, and a set-aside does not restore firearm rights under
current OSP policy. The agent must verify the current statutory text, the waiting-period tiers, the eligible/excluded offense lists,
OSP request processes, and county procedures before relying on any rule here.

Oregon Set-Aside (Expungement) — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase
